blocks.title: By the end of this module, you will be able to:
items: Participate actively and collaboratively in the initial conduct of a mathematical investigation
Pose problems related to the situation identified by the group
Participate actively and collaboratively in formulating conjectures
Formulate conjectures gathered from gathered data
Participate actively and collaboratively in verifying conjectures
Verify conjectures using an appropriate verification method
Participate actively and collaboratively in justifying conjectures
Prove conjectures using an appropriate method of proving
Participate actively and collaboratively in formulating the summary and possible extensions of their MI paper
Participate actively in the MI Congress

blocks.title: Foundations of Collaborative Mathematical Investigation
items: Diverse Perspectives — different group members see different aspects
Cognitive Synergy — group thinking that surpasses individual capabilities
Social Learning — learning mathematical communication and argumentation skills
Real-World Preparation — mirrors how mathematicians actually work

blocks.html:
Choosing Investigation Topics:

  • Personal interest and curiosity
  • Availability of resources
  • Mathematical significance
  • Real-world relevance

Example Starter: Investigate the Fibonacci sequence in nature.

Close-ended: Single correct answer.
Example: "Solve 2x + 5 = 15"

Open-ended: Multiple approaches and solutions.
Example: "Find as many ways as possible to make 50 using operations and digits 2, 3, 4"

Formulating Clear Research Questions:

  • Specific and focused
  • Mathematically meaningful
  • Investigable with available resources

Example: Instead of "Study triangles," use "Investigate the relationship between triangle side lengths and area."
Systematic Data Collection: Tables and charts, systematic experimentation, careful measurement and recording.

blocks.caption: Example: Investigating Rectangle Areas with Fixed Perimeter (P = 20)

blocks.html:
Developing Testable Hypotheses: Based on observed patterns, clear and specific statements, mathematically precise.

Example: For rectangles with fixed perimeter, the area is maximized when the rectangle is a square.

Testing with Multiple Cases: Try various examples, look for counterexamples, consider edge cases.

Example: Test the rectangle conjecture with perimeters 12, 16, 20, etc.

Developing Mathematical Proofs: Logical reasoning, algebraic justification, geometric arguments.

Rectangle Justification: Let perimeter P = 2(l + w), so w = P/2 − l. Area A = l × w = l(P/2 − l) = (P/2)l − l². This is a quadratic, maximum at l = P/4, so w = P/4 → square.

sections.heading: 4.8 Writing the Summary and Possible Extensions

blocks.html:
Structuring the Investigation Report:

  • Introduction and problem statement
  • Methodology and data collection
  • Findings and conjectures
  • Justification and proofs
  • Conclusions and reflections
  • Extensions and new questions

Example Extensions: "What about 3D shapes? What if we fix surface area instead of perimeter?"

blocks.title: Written Report Guide (Approx. 3-5 pages)
items: Title & Introduction: State your investigation question and its origins
The Process: Detail your exploration. What strategies did you use? Include your tables, charts, and failed attempts — this is key!
Findings & Conjectures: Clearly state the patterns you found and the conjectures you made
Justification & Proof: Provide your reasoning for why your conjectures are true. This can be logical argument, visual proof, or algebraic proof
Conclusions & Reflections: Summarize your main discovery. What was most challenging? What new questions arose?
Extensions: Suggest one or two ways this investigation could be taken further

blocks.title: Presentation Development
items: Audience Analysis: Tailoring presentation for mathematical peers
Visual Aid Design: Creating clear, engaging presentation materials
Time Management: Practicing within time constraints (10-12 minutes)
Q&A Preparation: Anticipating and preparing for questions

blocks.title: Presentation Tips
items: Hook the audience with your initial question
Briefly show your process of discovery
Clearly state your main conjecture and your explanation for it
Conclude with your most interesting reflection or extension
Use visual aids (poster, slides, whiteboard) effectively

blocks.html: The following are 25 suggested investigation topics for students to explore:

blocks.title: Investigation Topics
items: 1. Happy Numbers: Square each digit and add the squares repeatedly. If sequence reaches 1, the number is 'happy'; otherwise 'sad.' Investigate.
2. Lines: Lines are drawn on a plane. Investigate.
3. Ants: Ants emerge from their hole at the top of a wire grid and always walk downwards. Investigate the paths they may take.
4. Consecutive Sums: Some numbers can be expressed as the sum of consecutive positive integers. 9=2+3+4, 11=5+6, 18=3+4+5+6. Investigate.
5. Returns: A robot walks one pace and turns 90° right, two paces and turns 90° right, three paces and turns 90° right, repeating. Investigate.
6. Birthdays: On what day of the week will your 100th birthday fall? Investigate this and other days of interest.
7. Corners: A figure has two different types of corners. Investigate.
8. Triangles: Draw triangles with sides of integral length and longest side of length 5 units. Investigate.
9. Rectangles: How many rectangles are there in a diagram? Investigate.
10. Piles: Start with two unequal piles of coins. Shift enough coins from the larger pile to double the smaller. Continue. Investigate.
11. Sums of Squares: Some numbers can be written as sums of squares. Investigate.
12. Primes: Look at a table of prime numbers. How are primes spread through the counting numbers? Investigate.
13. Co-primes: Two positive integers are co-prime if their GCF is 1. The four positive integers less than 10 co-prime with 10 are 1,3,7,9. Investigate.
14. Tiles: Two small squares are printed on a square grid. Can the remainder be covered with 2×1 tiles? Investigate.
15. Straws: Squares are made from colored straws. Investigate.
16. Coins: Put coins all heads up on a table. Turning over three at a time, try to get all tails up. Investigate.
17. Strips: Make a strip of squares. Color some whole squares black, leaving others white. Investigate.
18. Repeats: Unit fractions have a numerator of 1. Investigate those whose decimal equivalents recur.
19. Joins: Each of three points can be joined to the other two without any joins crossing. Investigate for more than three points.
20. Chords: Mark points on a circle and join them by chords. Investigate.
21. Stairs: Consider stairs made up of n layers. Investigate.
22. Layers of Equilateral Triangles: Draw equilateral triangles with x layers. Investigate.
23. Handshakes: There are n people at a party and each shakes hands with others. Investigate.
24. Diagonals: Diagonals of regular polygons are connected. Investigate.
25. Billiard Balls: Make a billiard ball triangle of varying layers and varying ball radii. Investigate.

blocks.title: Activity 1: Final Mathematical Investigation Paper (25 points)

rows.criteria: Introduction (3 pts) — Investigation question and motivation
Methodology (4 pts) — Collaborative process, data collection, challenges
Findings and Analysis (8 pts) — Patterns, conjectures, comprehensive analysis
ratings: Insightful patterns, clear conjectures, thorough analysis

rows.criteria: Justification and Proof (6 pts) — Mathematical reasoning, verification approaches
Conclusion and Extensions (4 pts) — Summary, reflection, new questions

questions.text: What is the main benefit of collaboration in mathematical investigation?
options: A) Makes mathematics easier
B) Allows dividing work to finish faster
C) Brings diverse perspectives and creates cognitive synergy
D) Reduces work each student must do

questions.text: When choosing an investigation topic, what should be the PRIMARY consideration?
options: A) How quickly it can be completed
B) Personal interest and mathematical significance
C) Whether the teacher approves
D) How similar it is to textbook problems

questions.text: Which is an example of an open-ended problem?
options: A) Solve 3x+7=22
B) Calculate area of circle radius 5
C) Find as many patterns as possible in Pascal's Triangle
D) Multiply 15×24

questions.text: What characterizes a well-formulated research question?
options: A) Broad and general
B) Specific, focused, and mathematically meaningful
C) Simple enough to solve in one step
D) Similar to textbook questions

questions.text: In the rectangle area investigation with fixed perimeter, what pattern was discovered?
options: A) Area increases as length increases
B) Area is maximized when the rectangle is a square
C) Area decreases as width increases
D) Area remains constant regardless of dimensions

questions.text: Which method was used to justify the rectangle area conjecture?
options: A) Asking multiple people
B) Algebraic manipulation showing quadratic optimization
C) Testing only with perimeter of 20
D) Drawing pictures of rectangles

questions.text: What is the purpose of the 'Extensions' section in an investigation report?
options: A) List all references
B) Suggest new questions and related investigations
C) Summarize what was done
D) Acknowledge people who helped

questions.text: When verifying conjectures, what is MOST important?
options: A) Only test cases that support the conjecture
B) Look for counterexamples and test edge cases
C) Ask the teacher if correct
D) Assume it is true after a few tests

questions.text: What does 'cognitive synergy' in collaboration refer to?
options: A) Group thinking that surpasses individual capabilities
B) Dividing work equally
C) Taking turns speaking
D) Smartest person does the work

questions.text: Which is the BEST research question for investigation?
options: A) Study triangles
B) Investigate relationship between triangle side lengths and area
C) Learn about geometry
D) What is a triangle?

questions.text: What is the key difference between close-ended and open-ended problems?
options: A) Close-ended are harder
B) Open-ended have multiple approaches and solutions
C) Close-ended require more creativity
D) Open-ended always have right answers

questions.text: When organizing data systematically, what is the MAIN benefit of using tables?
options: A) They look more professional
B) They help identify patterns and relationships clearly
C) They are required by teachers
D) They take up more space

questions.text: What makes a conjecture mathematically precise?
options: A) Uses complex language
B) Stated clearly and can be tested
C) Written in paragraph form
D) Includes many examples

questions.text: In the MI Congress, what should the opening of a presentation do?
options: A) List all formulas used
B) Thank everyone for coming
C) Hook the audience with the initial question
D) Read the entire paper aloud

questions.text: What is the purpose of including 'failed attempts' in the investigation report?
options: A) Show how much work was done
B) Demonstrate the learning process and exploration
C) Fill space
D) Explain why it was difficult

questions.text: Why test the rectangle conjecture with different perimeters?
options: A) To make investigation take longer
B) To ensure the pattern holds in multiple cases
C) Because the teacher requires it
D) To have more data for tables

questions.text: What is the MAIN goal of 'Conclusions and Reflections'?
options: A) Summarize discovery and reflect on the process
B) List all formulas used
C) Thank everyone who helped
D) Suggest topics for future students

questions.text: In collaborative conjecture formulation, what should members do FIRST?
options: A) Vote on which is probably correct
B) Have each member identify potential patterns individually
C) Let smartest person decide
D) Look up the answer in a textbook

questions.text: What does 'audience analysis' involve for the MI Congress?
options: A) Counting attendees
B) Tailoring the presentation for mathematical peers
C) Preparing gifts
D) Choosing the best time

questions.text: Why consider 'edge cases' when verifying conjectures?
options: A) They are usually most interesting
B) They often reveal limitations of the conjecture
C) They make verification faster
D) Required for full credit

questions.text: What is the primary value of 'real-world preparation' in collaborative investigation?
options: A) Makes math more enjoyable
B) Mirrors how mathematicians actually work
C) Helps get better grades
D) Allows using calculators

questions.text: When finalizing the paper, what should be checked for proper mathematical notation?
options: A) All formulas, symbols, equations are correct and clear
B) Has enough colorful diagrams
C) Font size is readable
D) No grammar errors

questions.text: What is the BEST approach for Q&A preparation for the MI Congress?
options: A) Memorize possible questions and answers
B) Anticipate questions and prepare thoughtful responses
C) Hope no one asks difficult questions
D) Ask friends to only ask easy questions

questions.text: In rectangle area optimization, what concept explains why area is maximized as a square?
options: A) Quadratic functions have maximum values at their vertices
B) Circles have max area for given perimeter
C) All rectangles have same area
D) Triangles always have less area

questions.text: What does 'social learning' in collaborative investigation refer to?
options: A) Learning mathematical communication and argumentation skills
B) Making friends during group work
C) Sharing answers with other groups
D) Using social media for research

questions.text: After brainstorming questions, what should the group do?
options: A) Immediately investigate all questions
B) Categorize and prioritize which questions to investigate
C) Ask the teacher which is best
D) Choose the easiest question

questions.text: What is the key element of active participation in collaborative work?
options: A) Always agreeing with consensus
B) Contributing ideas and building on others' suggestions
C) Letting others do the work
D) Working silently to avoid conflict

questions.text: Why is visual aid design important for MI Congress presentations?
options: A) To create clear, engaging presentation materials
B) To use most advanced technology
C) To make presentation longer
D) To impress judges with graphics

questions.text: What should the Methodology section include?
options: A) Only successful strategies
B) All approaches tried, including unsuccessful ones
C) A list of formulas
D) Biographies of group members

questions.text: What is the ultimate goal of the Mathematical Investigation Congress?
options: A) To win awards
B) To share mathematical discoveries and learn from peers
C) To finish the requirement
D) To prove your group is smartest
